title: Throwing a stoneware bottle form
subtitle: A wheel-thrown closed-form bottle with a narrow neck
excerpt: A wheel-thrown stoneware bottle with a closed shoulder and a narrow neck. The bottle form requires collaring the clay inward to close the opening, a move that distinguishes it from open-cylinder throwing. Thrown from 600 g of smooth stoneware. Requires a potter's wheel and kiln access to cone 6.
sourceNotes: Bottle-form throwing technique derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for historical bottle form reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Squeezing the clay inward with both hands around the neck to narrow the opening of a thrown form.
Settling a clay mound on the wheel head so it rotates without wobbling.
Drawing clay upward between fingers to thin and raise the wall of a thrown pot.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim.
The trimmed-back base ridge a finished pot sits on.
The first firing at cone 06 that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware.
A pyrometric measure of heat-work. Cone 6 is roughly 1220°C.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio.

: squeezing the clay inward to close the shoulder of the pot and narrow the neck. This is the move that opens cylinder-throwing into full three-dimensional form-making. The bottle is thrown from 600 g of smooth stoneware. Kiln access to

600 g requires a solid attachment to the bat. Wet the bat and slap the clay on firmly before starting the wheel. The bottle form uses more clay than a cup: keep hands wetter than usual throughout the throwing.

Centre the mound and open to 80 mm diameter. Pull the cylinder wall in three pulls, reaching 160 mm tall with walls 6-7 mm thick. The base should be 10-12 mm thick. At this stage the form is a straight cylinder.
complete when the cylinder stands vertical with even walls.

With the wheel at slow to medium speed, place one hand inside and the other outside at the lower-to-middle section. Press outward with the inside fingers while the outside hand provides light support. The belly of the bottle expands outward at the mid-section. Work up the form in short strokes, nudging the clay outward. The widest point should be at about half the bottle height, reaching 130-140 mm diameter.

Collaring brings the wall back inward above the belly to form the shoulder. Place both hands around the clay at the upper-third of the form. Apply gentle inward squeezing pressure with both hands simultaneously as the wheel turns. The clay moves inward and upward. Move the hands upward slowly in a series of short collaring strokes, each narrowing the form by about 10 mm. The opening narrows from 80 mm to about 30-35 mm for the neck.
Between each collaring stroke, allow the wheel to make one full revolution with no pressure. This releases the twist the clay takes during collaring and prevents the form from spiralling out of true.

Once the neck is at 30-35 mm diameter, pull the neck upward in one or two final pulls to extend it to about 40 mm tall. The neck walls should be about 5 mm thick. Compress the rim with the chamois to close any cracks and create a clean edge. Trim the rim level with the needle tool if it is uneven.
Wire the bottle off the bat. Dry slowly under a plastic cover for 24 hours. At
with the loop tool. The foot ring on a bottle form is typically 6-8 mm wide and 10-12 mm tall, giving the bottle a stable base.
at cone 06. Glaze the exterior only if the bottle is decorative; glaze interior and exterior if it will be used to hold liquid.
Throw a smaller 300 g bottle with a narrower neck, 20 mm diameter, for a bud vase form.
Throw a double-gourd bottle by collaring twice at different heights to create two bellied sections.
Decorate with sgraffito at the leather-hard stage: apply a slip coat, then carve through it before bisque firing.

rows.problem: Neck spirals out of true during collaring
rows.cause: Collaring pressure applied continuously without releasing between strokes
rows.fix: Release pressure and let the wheel complete a full revolution between each collaring stroke.

rows.cause: Walls were too thin before attempting to belly the form outward
rows.fix: Keep the walls at 6-7 mm after the cylinder pulls before bellying. Thin walls cannot support outward pressure.

rows.cause: Neck walls were too thin, or clay was too wet when form was left to dry
rows.fix: Leave the bottle on the bat until the form is firm enough to hold the shoulder shape independently.
